Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of just study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Most weeks I'd squeeze in maybe 13 hours total, but it was scattered and unplanned. I'm a visual learner so I'd watch videos when I could, but organizing a proper schedule never really happened for me.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was going on. If there was a deadline coming up, I'd suddenly be more active, but otherwise I'd drift and not really commit to regular times. Some weeks I barely touched anything, other weeks I'd cram. It was pretty inconsistent, and I know that's not ideal but that's just how things went.
Q3: Usually it was because something was due or I got a notification about an assignment. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing something important. I wouldn't say I ever logged in because I was excited to learn—it was more about not falling too far behind. Anxiety about missing deadlines pushed me more than actual interest.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, but it never stuck as a habit. I think around midterms I logged in more consistently for a week or two, but then I'd fall off again. It just never became automatic for me like it probably should have.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I learn better visually. I watched over 200 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and actually see concepts explained. The discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better than the lectures.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the optional quizzes. Without internet at home, accessing everything was a hassle, so I'd prioritize what seemed most important. If something looked text-heavy or boring, I'd skip it or tell myself I'd get to it later, which usually meant never.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. Visual stuff that was easy to follow also felt more worthwhile. But if something seemed abstract or I couldn't figure out why it mattered, I'd lose interest fast and move on to something else.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had consequences if I didn't do them, so I'd at least attempt those. Optional ones just fell to the bottom of my priority list because with my low motivation, I needed that external pressure to actually do anything.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download videos and materials when I had access because I don't have internet at home. Then I'd study from those files offline. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me with downloaded content on my laptop at home.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through downloaded lecture videos and my notes, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd pause videos constantly to write things down, really trying to understand, but the system would only show that I watched something once. My offline studying was invisible.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times at home without internet. Same with PDFs—I'd grab them and study from them for days. The LMS only captured that initial download, not all the actual learning that happened after.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when my anxiety spiked. Sometimes I'd feel overwhelmed looking at everything I needed to do and just shut down instead of engaging. When assignments piled up or I felt confused about material, I'd avoid the LMS entirely for days.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue hit me hard some weeks, and with limited resources and no internet at home, everything felt like more effort than it should be. When I was tired or stressed about other life stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Competing responsibilities always won.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but I wasn't really absorbing anything. My mind would wander, especially when I was anxious. I'd technically be logged in and active, but mentally I was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety would build up and instead of tackling things, I'd avoid them. It's like the more important something was, the more scared I felt about messing it up, so I'd just... not start. It's a bad cycle I'm aware of.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff until the deadline was close, then suddenly I'd be logging in frantically trying to complete things. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it, but I didn't always check it right away. Sometimes seeing a decent grade would motivate me briefly, but bad feedback would make me want to avoid the course even more. It was hit or miss whether feedback actually changed my behavior.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit overwhelming with five online courses going at once. I couldn't keep track of everything properly. If things were more spaced out or simpler, I might have engaged more consistently. The structure kind of assumed I had more time and resources than I actually did.
Q19: Having videos helped me a lot since I'm visual, so that part of the design worked. But the amount of content and the pace made it harder. Without reliable internet, the expectation that I'd be constantly online was tough. More downloadable, offline-friendly materials would have helped.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's anxious and keeps checking things but can't bring themselves to actually submit work. You might be logging in, watching stuff, reading forums, but the actual assignments feel too intimidating to complete. High activity doesn't mean high productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I passed the course despite not being super active on the LMS because I did a lot of studying offline with downloaded materials. My exam score was decent enough. The LMS doesn't capture everything, so low activity doesn't always mean low learning.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which gives a basic picture. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and what's going on in your head. They can't tell if you're anxious, distracted, or actually deeply focused. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real mental effort, not just showing up. It's when the material sticks and you want to understand it. For me, I was only truly engaged in brief moments—mostly I was just trying to survive and pass.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety or how much offline studying I did. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises that actually helped my mood improve by the end. It also wouldn't show that despite my low motivation, I was genuinely trying in my own limited way.
